summary: Argue whether celebrity endorsements help or harm democratic participation in local elections, and consider the structural distortions they create.
topic: The case against celebrity endorsements in local elections

title: Whether religious sites should charge tourists
summary: Argue whether religious sites — cathedrals, temples, mosques, monasteries — are justified in charging admission fees to tourists, and what consequences follow either way.
topic: Whether religious sites should charge tourists

title: The ethics of accepting freebies as a journalist
summary: Argue whether journalists can accept free meals, trips, or products from companies they cover, and what this means for editorial independence.
topic: The ethics of accepting freebies as a journalist

title: Whether subtitles should translate slang literally or culturally
summary: Argue whether subtitle translators should render slang and colloquial expressions literally, or adapt them to culturally equivalent expressions in the target language.
topic: Whether subtitles should translate slang literally or culturally

title: Whether city councils should commission public art without a public vote
summary: Argue whether the decision to commission public art should belong to expert panels and elected officials, or require a public vote before any work is installed.
topic: Whether city councils should commission public art without a public vote

title: The case for municipal composting over individual recycling
summary: Argue whether cities should invest in centralised municipal composting systems rather than relying on individual household recycling as the primary strategy for reducing organic waste.
topic: The case for municipal composting over individual recycling

title: Attention as a moral resource
summary: Argue that attention — what we choose to notice, sustain, and offer to others — is not merely a cognitive capacity but a moral one, with ethical stakes comparable to time or money.
topic: Attention as a moral resource

title: On preserving what is dying: when is letting go the more honest choice?
summary: Argue that letting go can be more honest than preserving — that some acts of preservation serve the preserver rather than the thing preserved — using two contrasting examples and a careful concession.
topic: When letting go is more honest than preserving

title: The quiet violence of being unrecognised at work
summary: Argue the harm of being chronically unrecognised at work, show it through two concrete cases, and propose what an honest remedy would actually require — including what it would cost those in power to provide it.
topic: Being unrecognised at work — the harm and the remedy

title: The architecture of being noticed
summary: Argue that cities physically and procedurally distribute visibility, and that this distribution is unjust.
topic: The architecture of being noticed

title: On apology as labor
summary: Argue that apology, especially in service work, is a form of unpaid emotional labor — and consider what acknowledging that would change.
topic: On apology as labor

title: The phenomenology of forgetting on purpose
summary: Argue that deliberate forgetting is a discipline of attention, not a failure of memory.
topic: The phenomenology of forgetting on purpose

title: Against the moral framing of rest
summary: Critique the productivity ideology that treats rest as recovery for work, and defend rest as its own end.
topic: Against the moral framing of rest

title: The hidden grammar of class in the language we use to thank people
summary: Examine how expressions of gratitude encode social hierarchy, and argue that the form of a thank-you reveals as much as its content.
topic: How gratitude language encodes class and hierarchy

title: On loyalty to projects that have outgrown their original meaning
summary: Argue that continuing to serve a project, institution, or cause after it has outgrown its founding purpose raises distinct moral and practical questions about loyalty.
topic: Loyalty to projects that have exceeded or changed their original meaning

title: When inheritance becomes coercion: the moral weight of received traditions
summary: Examine the line between preserving a received cultural tradition and imposing it, and argue that inheritance carries a moral obligation of consent.
topic: When received cultural traditions become coercive impositions

title: The aesthetics of the imperfect: why some craft cultures refuse polish
summary: Examine the deliberate embrace of imperfection in certain craft and design traditions, and argue that this refusal of polish is not nostalgia but a distinct aesthetic and ethical position.
topic: Why some craft traditions deliberately embrace imperfection

title: On living with people who remember you differently than you remember yourself
summary: Examine the friction that arises when others hold a version of you — rooted in the past or in a different perspective — that you no longer recognise as yours.
topic: The friction of being remembered differently than you remember yourself

title: The ethics of writing about a place you no longer live in
summary: Examine the moral tensions that arise when writers depict homes, cities, or countries they have left — and argue that the distance required to write clearly about a place is also the distance that distorts it.
topic: Whether writers who have left a place retain the authority — or bear the responsibility — to represent it

title: Against the cult of intention in art and design
summary: Challenge the assumption that knowing what an artist or designer intended is the most illuminating — or most relevant — way to evaluate their work.
topic: Whether authorial intention should govern how we judge art and designed objects

title: The political economy of being on time
summary: Examine how punctuality is not a neutral virtue but a practice shaped by power, infrastructure, and whose time is deemed worth protecting.
topic: How punctuality encodes social power and whose time is treated as valuable

title: On gentleness as a discipline rather than a temperament
summary: Argue that gentleness is not a personality trait one either has or lacks, but a practice that must be cultivated against the grain of competitive social life.
topic: Whether gentleness is an achieved discipline or simply a natural disposition

title: When honesty becomes a form of self-display
summary: Examine the phenomenon of conspicuous candour — the performance of honesty that serves the speaker's self-image more than the listener's need for truth.
topic: Whether radical honesty is always an ethical act or sometimes a form of self-indulgence

title: The half-life of a good idea inside an institution
summary: Argue that good ideas introduced into institutions tend to lose their transformative force over time — not through sabotage, but through the ordinary processes of bureaucratisation, absorption, and reinterpretation.
topic: Why good ideas lose their force once absorbed by institutions

title: On the moral status of attention you cannot give back
summary: Argue that drawing someone's sustained attention — in education, media, or relationships — carries a moral obligation, because attention is a finite and non-renewable resource.
topic: The moral weight of claiming and using other people's attention

title: Against the metaphor of 'balance' in working life
summary: Argue that the dominant metaphor of 'work-life balance' misrepresents the actual structure of a working life by implying a static equilibrium between separable quantities — and that better metaphors are available and necessary.
topic: Why 'work-life balance' is a misleading and ultimately harmful metaphor

title: When the language of healing harms the people it was meant to help
summary: Argue that therapeutic language — originally designed to reduce stigma and support vulnerable people — can, when misapplied or over-extended, cause harm by pathologising ordinary experience, reducing moral agency, and crowding out more adequate responses.
topic: How therapeutic language can harm as well as help

title: On the dignity of leaving things unsaid
summary: Argue that restraint in speech — the deliberate choice not to say everything one could say — is a form of moral and communicative intelligence, not reticence or dishonesty, and that the contemporary pressure toward total expressivity erodes the conditions for trust.
topic: The moral and relational value of deliberate silence and restraint in speech

title: The ethics of remembering someone better than they were
summary: Argue that the common practice of idealising the dead — softening their faults in memory and in speech — is neither simply dishonest nor simply kind, but raises genuine moral questions about the obligations of the living to the dead, to the truth, and to those still living.
topic: The moral complexity of idealising the dead in memory and speech

title: On the ethics of charisma in institutions that need quiet labor
summary: Examine how charisma functions as a form of informal power inside organizations, and argue that its rewards distort the recognition of slower, less visible contributions.
topic: How charismatic individuals extract disproportionate recognition from institutions built on unglamorous work

title: Against the language of 'investment' in human relationships
summary: Examine how the metaphor of financial investment has colonised the vocabulary of friendship, love, and care, and argue that this framing distorts what relationships are for.
topic: The distorting effects of economic metaphor on how we understand human relationships

title: The political asymmetry of who is allowed to be tired
summary: Examine how exhaustion is distributed unequally across social positions, and argue that who is permitted to express tiredness — and be taken seriously — is a question of power.
topic: How the social permission to express and be believed in exhaustion reflects and reinforces existing hierarchies

title: On the difficulty of being a witness without becoming a curator
summary: Examine the ethical tension between witnessing someone else's experience and the impulse to organise, interpret, and present it — and argue that this tension is not resolvable but must be held.
topic: The ethics of testimony, documentation, and the line between bearing witness and appropriating experience

title: The moral grammar of being on the inside of a decision
summary: Examine what changes morally when a person is on the inside of a consequential decision — not its object but one of its authors — and argue that proximity to power creates obligations that are not resolved by good intentions alone.
topic: The moral obligations that arise from participating in consequential institutional decisions

title: Against the framing of forgiveness as closure
summary: Challenge the therapeutic assumption that forgiveness necessarily resolves trauma or restores psychological wholeness — and argue that the pressure to forgive can itself be a second wound.
topic: Whether the cultural insistence on forgiveness as a path to closure places an unfair burden on those who have been harmed

title: On the discipline of refusing to be useful
summary: Examine the cultural pressure to justify one's time and existence through productivity, and argue that the capacity to resist usefulness — to think, rest, or create without instrumental purpose — is not a luxury but a condition of intellectual integrity.
topic: Whether the demand that individuals justify their activities through measurable usefulness diminishes human thought and life

title: When loyalty to a place outlives its hospitality to us
summary: Examine the emotional and moral complexity of continuing to love a city or country that has become hostile to certain of its inhabitants — and question whether such loyalty is devotion or complicity.
topic: Whether persisting attachment to a place that has turned hostile to one's identity constitutes meaningful loyalty or a form of self-erasure

title: The ethics of moral inheritance from people we cannot ask
summary: Examine the moral weight of inheriting ethical frameworks, loyalties, or obligations from deceased or unreachable predecessors — and argue whether such inheritance can be binding when no consent was given.
topic: Whether we can be bound by the moral commitments, debts, or loyalties of those who came before us when we had no say in inheriting them

title: Against the cult of clarity in difficult conversations
summary: Challenge the assumption that demanding clarity in emotionally charged or politically complex conversations is always an act of good faith — and argue that the pressure to 'be clear' can itself become a form of power over those who cannot speak plainly without risk.
topic: Whether the insistence on clarity in difficult conversations serves understanding or functions as a demand that certain speakers translate themselves into the language of the powerful

title: On the strange honesty of public failure
summary: Argue that public failure, precisely because it cannot be managed or reframed in the moment, produces a kind of disclosure that success and curated vulnerability rarely achieve.
topic: Whether public failure is a more honest form of self-disclosure than managed success or performed vulnerability

title: The political economy of who gets called late
summary: Argue that the distribution of late-night calls, emergency messages, and after-hours demands in organisations is not random but follows predictable social hierarchies, revealing who bears invisible costs that are never counted in official workload measures.
topic: Whether the informal distribution of after-hours demands in organisations is a form of structural inequality

title: When private grief is asked to perform in public rituals
summary: Argue that public mourning rituals — state funerals, commemorations, workplace memorial events — impose a performative demand on private grief that can distort, delay, or suppress authentic emotional processing.
topic: Whether public mourning rituals serve or obstruct those who are actually grieving

title: On the moral status of secrets we keep on others' behalf
summary: Argue that keeping a secret entrusted by another person is a moral act with genuine costs and complexities — not simply an exercise of loyalty, but a form of complicity whose ethical weight depends on the nature of the secret itself.
topic: Whether keeping another person's secret is always a morally neutral act of loyalty or sometimes a form of complicity

title: Against the metaphor of 'the journey' in professional life
summary: Argue that the pervasive use of 'the journey' as a metaphor for professional development is not merely a cliché but a conceptual distortion that misrepresents how careers actually unfold and subtly blames individuals for structural outcomes.
topic: Whether the 'journey' metaphor in professional development discourse is harmless or ideologically misleading

title: The ethics of inherited debts that no one named
summary: Explore the moral complexity of obligations passed down through families, institutions, or nations — debts incurred by others that nonetheless arrive at your door unnamed and unanticipated.
topic: The moral weight of obligations you did not incur but cannot refuse

title: On living near people whose ordinariness you envy
summary: Examine the experience of envying not another person's achievements or wealth, but the quieter texture of their daily life — and what that envy reveals about one's own conception of the good.
topic: The ethics and phenomenology of envying ordinary contentment

title: When institutional kindness becomes a form of containment
summary: Examine the line between genuine care offered by institutions — schools, hospitals, social services — and the form of control that can be enacted through the very language and gestures of kindness.
topic: The boundary between institutional care and institutional control

title: Against the assumption that listening is passive
summary: Argue that genuine listening is an active, demanding, and ethically laden practice — and that the cultural assumption of its passivity devalues both the act and the person performing it.
topic: The active and ethical dimensions of attentive listening

title: On the dignity of disagreements that don't resolve
summary: Argue that unresolved disagreements can be a mark of intellectual health rather than failure — and that the compulsion to reach consensus too quickly often sacrifices truth for comfort.
topic: The value of disagreements that persist without resolution

title: The case against legibility: when institutions work better unmeasured
summary: Argue that the impulse to make every institution legible to outside measurement can damage the very practices that make institutions work — and that some forms of opacity are protective rather than evasive.
topic: The hidden costs of making institutions fully measurable

title: On the moral economy of the corner shop in a city of chains
summary: Argue that the disappearance of the independent corner shop is not merely a commercial event but the loss of a small civic infrastructure whose moral functions chains do not replicate.
topic: What is lost when chain stores replace the independent neighbourhood shop

title: Against the cult of authenticity in personal writing
summary: Argue that the demand for 'authentic' personal writing has become its own kind of performance — and that craft, distance, and even a degree of artifice can serve truth better than a confession produced on demand.
topic: Why 'be authentic' is the wrong instruction for personal writing

title: On the obligation to remember languages that are no longer profitable
summary: Argue that the survival of a minority language is not the responsibility of the market or the school inspector but a collective ethical obligation — and that 'usefulness' is the wrong axis on which to judge it.
topic: Whether and why we owe something to languages that the market does not sustain

title: The hidden cost of professionalising what used to be neighbourly
summary: Argue that the steady professionalisation of work that was once done informally — care, food, repair, watching a neighbour's child — solves some problems while quietly creating others that the professional version cannot address.
topic: What is lost when informal mutual aid becomes a professional service

title: When restoration becomes erasure: the ethics of restoring old buildings
summary: Argue that the restoration of old buildings is not a neutral technical act — and that the choice between preservation, restoration, and reconstruction carries ethical weight about what we are willing to let a place mean.
topic: When does restoring a historic building become a form of erasing it?

title: On the dignity of refusing to translate yourself for a foreign audience
summary: Argue that the pressure on writers, artists, and ordinary people from non-dominant cultures to make themselves immediately legible to a foreign audience is a form of unequal labour — and that refusing it is sometimes the more honest choice.
topic: When the demand for legibility to foreign audiences becomes a form of inequality

title: Against the framing of nostalgia as escape
summary: Argue that nostalgia, properly understood, is not a retreat from the present but a form of moral attention to what has been lost — and that dismissing it as escapism is itself an evasion.
topic: Reconsidering nostalgia as a serious form of attention

title: The political life of footpaths and shortcuts
summary: Argue that the unofficial paths people make through a city — the desire lines worn across grass, the shortcuts through abandoned lots — are a quiet vote on the failures of urban planning, and that to pave them is sometimes more honest than to fence them off.
topic: What the paths people make for themselves reveal about planned space

title: On being asked your opinion when you have no power to act on it
summary: Examine the strange ethical position of being consulted on decisions whose outcome one cannot affect — and argue that the right response is neither cynical silence nor performed enthusiasm but a careful, limited kind of engagement.
topic: What we owe to consultations whose outcomes we cannot influence

title: The case for formality between people who know each other well
summary: Argue that the steady replacement of formal address and small ceremony with constant casualness, even among intimates, has costs that are easier to dismiss than to defend — and that some formality between known people is a form of respect, not distance.
topic: Why even close relationships benefit from a small measure of formality

title: When a craft outlives its market: the ethics of teaching a dying skill
summary: Argue that the decision to keep teaching a craft whose commercial future has collapsed is not foolish romanticism — it is a quiet ethical act that preserves the option of recovery for a future that may yet need it.
topic: What we owe to crafts the market no longer rewards

title: On the moral risk of explaining a place to outsiders
summary: Argue that the person who explains a place to those who do not live there carries a small ethical responsibility — the city they describe will be the city the outsiders believe in, and the description will return, eventually, to change the place itself.
topic: The responsibilities of explaining a place to outsiders

title: Against the metaphor of 'community' in commercial contexts
summary: Argue that the casual use of 'community' by platforms, brands, and workplaces to describe their customers or workforce empties the word of its older meaning — and that the result is not flattery but a quiet form of misrecognition.
topic: What is lost when the word 'community' is borrowed by commerce

title: The strange ethics of borrowed authority
summary: Examine the position of people who, lacking authority of their own, speak with the borrowed authority of an institution — and argue that this borrowed standing carries obligations the borrower rarely notices.
topic: What we owe when we speak with authority that is not ours

title: On the dignity of work that produces nothing visible
summary: Argue that work whose result cannot be photographed — care, mediation, maintenance, listening — is consistently undervalued precisely because it leaves no display object, and that recognising this requires more than a campaign for thanks.
topic: What we systematically miss when we value work by its visible output

title: When inconvenience is a feature, not a bug: the value of friction in civic life
summary: Argue that the systematic removal of friction from civic life — queueing, paperwork, the meeting one must attend in person — sometimes removes more than the inconvenience it was meant to eliminate.
topic: The civic value of certain inconveniences worth preserving

title: On the moral weight of small permissions we grant ourselves
summary: Examine the slow, almost imperceptible way that a long sequence of small self-permissions — each defensible on its own — accumulates into a character one would not have chosen if asked all at once.
topic: How small self-permissions accumulate into character

title: Against the modernisation of mourning
summary: Argue that the steady tidying-up of grief practices — shorter funerals, optional rituals, the privatisation of bereavement — has removed shared structures that protected the bereaved without anyone having decided to remove them.
topic: What has been lost as the rituals around death have been quietly streamlined

title: On owing something to the people who will live where we live now
summary: Argue that the inhabitants of a place owe a quiet obligation to those who will live in it after them — and that this obligation, while diffuse, places real limits on what we may do with the place in our own lifetime.
topic: What we owe to the future inhabitants of the places we currently live in

title: On the ethics of admiring a thinker whose conduct was poor
summary: Argue that the question of whether we may still admire a thinker whose private conduct was indefensible is harder, and more interesting, than either the impulse to cancel or the impulse to separate work from life will admit.
topic: Whether — and how — we may admire a thinker whose conduct was poor

title: The case for occasional public refusals
summary: Argue that the public refusal — the visible 'no' delivered without disclaimers — has its own civic and moral value, and that a culture in which everyone says yes by default loses something important about the meaning of consent.
topic: The dignity and civic function of saying 'no' in public

title: When neutrality becomes a position
summary: Argue that the professional ideal of impartiality — practised by judges, journalists, mediators, doctors, civil servants — is itself a position, and that pretending otherwise produces a specific category of moral injury.
topic: The ethics of professional impartiality

title: On the social function of complaint that nobody acts on
summary: Argue that ritualised, unactioned complaint — about weather, government, transport, prices, neighbours — performs real social work, and that a society that grows impatient with it loses something more delicate than first appears.
topic: What ritual complaint does that nobody seems to notice

title: The politics of who gets called by their first name
summary: Argue that the seemingly trivial question of who is addressed by their first name, and by whom, encodes a politics that is older and more consequential than any explicit hierarchy in the room.
topic: Address, first names, and the hidden distribution of authority

title: Why some institutions need to be small to be honest
summary: Argue that certain institutions — schools, congregations, journals, clinics, neighbourhood associations — lose their constitutive honesty when they pass a threshold of size, and that the loss is not a question of management but of form.
topic: Scale and the loss of institutional honesty

title: On the moral risk of being the person who explains a tradition
summary: Argue that whoever takes it upon themselves to explain a tradition — to outsiders, to descendants, to the curious — assumes a quiet authorship that is rarely acknowledged, and that this authorship has real costs whether the explainer wants them or not.
topic: The hidden authorship of those who explain traditions to others

title: The case against optimising childhood
summary: Argue that the contemporary impulse to optimise a child's hours, friendships, exposures, and rest is doing a particular kind of damage that the optimisers themselves will not be able to see for some years.
topic: Why childhood resists optimisation

title: On the ethical asymmetry of giving and receiving advice
summary: Argue that the moral situations of the adviser and the advised are not symmetrical, that the asymmetry is rarely acknowledged, and that ignoring it leads to a particular kind of harm done with the best of intentions.
topic: The unequal moral positions of adviser and advised

title: The dignity of jobs that involve waiting
summary: Argue that work whose principal substance is waiting — security guards, receptionists, on-call clinicians, ferry operators in slow seasons, the long second shift of the night porter — has a kind of dignity that the productivity vocabulary cannot register, and that the cost of not registering it is real.
topic: The dignity of jobs whose substance is being available

title: When discretion is more honest than transparency
summary: Argue that the modern presumption in favour of transparency, valuable as it is in many domains, becomes a kind of dishonesty when applied uncritically to situations whose moral substance depends on a degree of discretion.
topic: When transparency falsifies what discretion preserved

title: On the obligations of those who survive what others did not
summary: Argue that the survivor of a war, an epidemic, an industrial accident, or a slow institutional violence inherits a category of obligation that no one else can discharge for her — and that the public language of 'gratitude for being alive' is inadequate to its weight.
topic: What the survivor owes — to whom, and in what currency

title: The case for ritualised endings
summary: Argue that deliberate ceremonies of closure — the last day of a residency, the end of a season, the formal retirement — serve a cognitive and moral function that ordinary stopping does not.
topic: The value of formal, ritualised endings for seasons, terms, or residencies

title: Why some institutions should not have a vision statement
summary: Argue that the compulsory vision statement damages certain kinds of institutions by substituting aspiration for honesty, flattening internal disagreement, and committing an organisation to a self-image it may not deserve.
topic: The case against vision statements as a requirement for institutional seriousness

title: On the moral grammar of inheritance
summary: Examine the ethical logic of inheritance — what is owed to heirs, what is owed to others, and what the transfer of accumulated advantage says about the community that permits it.
topic: The ethics of inheritance and what it reveals about obligation, desert, and social arrangement

title: On the difference between a private life and a hidden one
summary: Argue that privacy and secrecy are morally distinct — that the right to a private life does not extend to concealment where others have a legitimate interest, and that confusing the two does serious damage.
topic: The moral distinction between privacy and secrecy, and the interests that each does or does not protect

title: When forgetting is a moral act
summary: Argue that there are circumstances in which the deliberate refusal to remember — or the conscious permission to forget — is ethically required rather than morally negligent.
topic: The ethical case for forgetting: when memory becomes an obstacle to justice or repair

title: On the philosophical poverty of 'authenticity' as a moral category
summary: Argue that 'authenticity' — the demand that people express their 'true self' — is a philosophically weak moral standard that often serves conformity rather than individuality, and confusion rather than clarity.
topic: The limitations of authenticity as a moral ideal and what it obscures about selfhood and obligation

title: On what cannot be delegated even when it could be
summary: Argue that there is a category of acts — caring for a dying parent, apologising in person, making a difficult decision — that retain their moral character only when performed by the person to whom they belong, even in a world where delegation is technically possible.
topic: The moral significance of acts that cannot be delegated without changing their meaning

title: The politics of who gets to be a beginner in public
summary: Argue that the social permission to learn in public — to be visibly incompetent on the way to competence — is unevenly distributed, and that this unevenness has political consequences that go beyond individual psychology.
topic: The unequal social permission to be a beginner in public and its political consequences
